critic_raw:
Hamiltonian Generative Networks claim to learn physically-grounded dynamics from image sequences of pendulums, spring-body systems, and two/three-body problems. The methodology has several underexamined assumptions.

The most fundamental claim is that the model learns the Hamiltonian — not just a good dynamics model. Learning the Hamiltonian implies that the learned latent energy function actually corresponds to the total energy of the system and that the symplectic integrator operates on correct canonical coordinates. But the variational encoder maps from pixel space to latent coordinates with no geometric constraint that those latent coordinates are canonical (position, momentum). If the encoder learns non-canonical coordinates, the HGN may learn a function that happens to produce good rollouts on training data without learning any Hamiltonian in the physics-relevant sense. Evidence needed: show that the learned energy function (evaluated at the ground-truth canonical coordinates if available for synthetic systems) matches the analytical Hamiltonian — not just that rollouts are visually accurate.

The forward/backward rollout test is used to demonstrate learned Hamiltonian structure, because true Hamiltonian systems are time-reversible. But near-perfect backward rollout can also be achieved by an approximately energy-conserving model that is not strictly Hamiltonian. The backward rollout metric needs a quantitative threshold (e.g., PSNR degradation rate per step) and comparison to an energy-conserving baseline (e.g., Neural ODE with energy regularization) that does not claim Hamiltonian structure. Without this, time-reversibility is not a distinguishing diagnostic.

The method is validated on 2-body and 3-body systems — the 3-body problem is chaotic, with Lyapunov divergence that makes long-term trajectory prediction inherently unreliable even with a perfect model. The evaluation metric for rollout quality must account for this: measuring pixel-level reconstruction error at long horizons on a chaotic system is dominated by exponential divergence, not model quality. The paper should report at what horizon the chaotic divergence begins to dominate the reconstruction error and distinguish this from model error.

Training on image sequences without ground-truth state information is the claimed strength, but it is also a source of unverifiable identifiability. For the pendulum, multiple (mass, length, gravity) combinations produce identical image sequences over short windows. The variational model may fit the image data perfectly while learning physically incorrect parameters; this is not detectable from image-domain metrics alone.
all_issues_raised: The paper claims to learn the Hamiltonian, but the variational encoder maps to unconstrained latent coordinates that need not be canonical (position, momentum); a model could produce accurate rollouts without learning any function that corresponds to the physical Hamiltonian.

Time-reversibility of rollouts is used as evidence of Hamiltonian structure, but near-perfect backward rollouts can also be achieved by approximately energy-conserving non-Hamiltonian models; no quantitative threshold or comparison baseline distinguishes these cases.

The 3-body problem is chaotic with exponential Lyapunov divergence; evaluating long-horizon rollout quality on a chaotic system conflates inherent physical unpredictability with model error, and the paper does not identify the horizon at which chaotic divergence dominates.

Training from image sequences without ground-truth state creates an identifiability problem: multiple physically distinct parameter combinations (mass, length, gravity) can produce identical short-window image sequences, so the model can fit observations while learning wrong physics.
all_issues_adjudicated: The paper claims to learn the Hamiltonian, but the variational encoder maps to unconstrained latent coordinates that need not be canonical (position, momentum); a model could produce accurate rollouts without learning any function that corresponds to the physical Hamiltonian.

Time-reversibility of rollouts is used as evidence of Hamiltonian structure, but near-perfect backward rollouts can also be achieved by approximately energy-conserving non-Hamiltonian models; no quantitative threshold or comparison baseline distinguishes these cases.

The 3-body problem is chaotic with exponential Lyapunov divergence; evaluating long-horizon rollout quality on a chaotic system conflates inherent physical unpredictability with model error, and the paper does not identify the horizon at which chaotic divergence dominates.

Training from image sequences without ground-truth state creates an identifiability problem: multiple physically distinct parameter combinations (mass, length, gravity) can produce identical short-window image sequences, so the model can fit observations while learning wrong physics.
